Gantt charts: a monument to optimism.
The chart was beautiful. So was the Titanic, once.
Standup meeting groans: Yesterday I worked. Today I will work. I have no blockers. (×15 people)
This standup has been going for 40 minutes. It is no longer a standup.
JIRA ticket laments: Ticket created. Into the backlog it goes.
PROJ-4821: needs more details. (Details never provided.)
The ticket is 'In Progress.' Has been since Q1.
Won't Fix. Works as Intended. Closed.
JIRA: where tasks go to live forever.
Release Friday warnings: It's 4:57 PM on Friday. Deploy confidently.
On-call is ready. The deploy is not.
This will be a quick deploy. (Famous last words.)
Hotfix horror stories: Quick fix. Two 3 AM calls later...
The hotfix introduced three new bugs.
We deployed the fix for the fix for the original fix.
Our hotfix process: push, pray, ping on-call.
The hotfix is now in production. So is the bug.
Legacy system \"features\": This has been working since 1997. Don't touch it.
Nobody knows what this does, but everything depends on it.
The legacy system is not broken. It is 'mature.'
There is no documentation. The author retired in 2003.
Feature: works perfectly for the use case nobody has anymore.
Technical debt metaphors: We'll pay it off next quarter. (We said this last quarter.)
Technical debt: the gift that keeps taking.
The interest rate on this code is compounding daily.
We built a house of cards on a foundation of spaghetti.
Refactoring procrastination: I'll refactor it when we have time. (We never have time.)
It works. That's good enough for now.
I left a TODO. It's been there three years.
The code is self-documenting. It says 'here be dragons.'
We'll clean this up in version 2.
Code review nitpicks: Should this variable be named 'data' or 'theData'?
Missing period at the end of this comment.
Can we align the assignment operators?
Merge conflict nightmares: The conflict is not in the code. It is in your soul.
Version control time travel: git log: a time machine to worse code.
git bisect: detective work for the truly masochistic.
Let me show you what this file looked like before it was ruined.
The history is clean. The code is not.
Git rebase confusion: I rebased and now nothing makes sense.
Interactive rebase: for when you want to rewrite history.
SVN vs Git nostalgia: In my day, we used SVN. And we were grateful.
SVN: one big trunk, many branches of regret.
At least SVN didn't have a detached HEAD.
git: SVN's overcomplicated cousin who gets all the jobs.
I still think about svn:externals. In therapy.
CVS \"ancient history\": CVS commit -m 'no idea what I broke'
CVS: the first version control system you learned to hate.
The CVS repository is read-only now. For everyone. Forever.
Before Git, there was darkness. It was called CVS.
CVS tags: a unique form of branch anxiety.
Open source license jokes: MIT: do whatever. Just don't blame me.
Apache 2.0: like MIT but more corporate.
Do What The F**k You Want To Public License: a bold choice.
GPL vs MIT banter: MIT: freedom to do what you want.
GPL: freedom to share what you take.
Fork it, MIT. Keep it, GPL.
Copyleft: because copying left is still copying right.
The license debate: where friendships go to die.
Stack trace poetry: StackOverflowError: for a website, this is ironic.
Exception in thread 'main': your assumptions were wrong.
Null pointer exceptions: Tony Hoare regrets inventing null. So does everyone else.
NullPointerException: it's always the null you didn't expect.
Check for null. Check again. Ship it.
The crash report says null. It's always null.
Off-by-one errors: Arrays start at 0. Or 1. Depending on the language and your grief.
Fence-post problem: count the posts, not the gaps.
Off by one: the error you only find in production.
for i in range(n): you wanted n+1, didn't you?
The loop ran 99 times. You needed 100.
Heisenbug sightings: The bug disappears when you add the print statement.
It works now. I changed nothing. My soul is uncertain.
The debugger makes it correct. The absence of the debugger does not.
Schrödinger's bug: both present and absent until observed.
Bohrbug vs Mandelbug: Bohrbug: solid, predictable, reproducible. Also rare.
Mandelbug: the more you look, the more complex it gets.
Heisenbug: see above. Or don't. It changes when you do.
Schrödinbug: works until you read the source code.
Some bugs resist classification. Those are the worst ones.
Rubber duck debugging: Tell the duck your problem. The duck doesn't judge.
The moment you explain it to someone, you see the answer.
The duck has fixed more bugs than any tool.
Step 1: find duck. Step 2: explain code. Step 3: fix bug. Duck optional.
Pair programming with a duck: often more productive than the alternative.
Cargo cult programming: The old code had it, so we kept it.
I don't know why this works, but removing it breaks everything.
We added a sleep(100) and the race condition went away. Probably.
This ritual was passed down by the senior dev who left.
The sacrifice of comments is made daily. The code remains inscrutable.
Yak shaving references: I was going to fix the bug, but first I had to set up the environment.
Setting up the environment required updating the toolchain.
Updating the toolchain required learning a new package manager.
Yak shaving: the noble art of doing everything except the thing.
How many yaks must be shaved before the feature ships?
Premature optimization warnings: Premature optimization is the root of all evil. (Knuth)
Make it work. Make it right. Make it fast. In that order.
This micro-optimization saved 2ms and cost 3 weeks.
The bottleneck was not where you thought it was.
\"Works on my machine\" syndrome: Have you tried running it on your machine?
The bug only exists in production.
My machine has 64GB RAM. Your container has 512MB. This explains things.
Code smell metaphors: This function does too much. It smells.
God object: knows everything, does everything, fixes nothing.
Long parameter list: a cry for help.
Dead code: the ghost of features past.
Spaghetti code jokes: This codebase is like pasta: tangled and cheesy.
The function calls itself, which calls another, which calls the first.
Control flow diagram: resembles a plate of linguine.
Spaghetti code serves no one, but feeds many bugs.
I refactored the spaghetti into... al dente spaghetti.
Lasagna code jokes: 100 layers deep. Each layer hides the one below.
Lasagna code: too many layers to see what's inside.
Layer 47: nobody knows what this does.
The abstraction is perfect. The result is inedible.
We have an interface for everything. Even the lasagna.
Pizza code jokes: Pizza code: flat, round, and everyone adds their own toppings.
Global state: the cheese on the pizza nobody ordered.
This module started as a one-topping pizza. Now it has everything.
Pizza code grows in every direction, no structure, unlimited toppings.
Pull request: adding a topping to the pizza you didn't make.
Copy-paste programming: This code was written by three developers and one StackExchange answer.
The bug was copy-pasted four times. Now it's a pattern.
I copied it from the example. The example was wrong.
Not invented here syndrome: We could use an existing library, but where's the fun in that?
We wrote our own ORM. It's almost like Hibernate. Except worse.
NIH: the allergy to other people's good ideas.
We don't use frameworks. We wrote our own. Twice.
The best code is the code I wrote myself. (This is incorrect.)
Reinventing the wheel: We needed a wheel, so we invented one. It's almost round.
Our wheel has fewer dependencies than the other wheel.
Wheel v1.0: technically works. v2.0: coming soon, we promise.
In hindsight, using a library would have taken one day.
Overengineering complaints: It's a simple button click. Why is there a message bus?
The microservice architecture for displaying 'Hello World' is complete.
We designed for 1 billion users. We have 12.
Future-proofing: solving problems that don't exist yet.
Abstraction so abstract, it abstracts the solution.
Underengineering regrets: I thought 32 bits would be enough. It was not.
We didn't need tests. Until we did.
The quick fix became the permanent solution.
Why did nobody add error handling?
There is no logging. We are flying blind.
Minimum viable product jokes: MVP: minimum viable product. Also: most victims possible.
Ship it. Let the users file the bugs.
Feature complete means: we ran out of time.
We launched! Now we fix the thing that was always broken.
The MVP is live. Now iterate. Forever.
Feature creep warnings: It was a simple text field. Now it has analytics.
The scope grew. Nobody knows how.
It started as a button. It is now a platform.
The product roadmap is a roadmap to a bigger roadmap.
Scope creep horror stories: While building the app, we also built an app to build apps.
The customer added 15 requirements during the demo.
Scope: was a sentence. Is now a novel.
We finished the original feature in week 2. We're still going.
Out of scope was crossed out in the contract.
Gold plating metaphors: The feature works. But have you considered making it golden?
We added animations nobody asked for.
This button has 14 visual states. Three were in scope.
Gold plating: improving things nobody wanted improved.
The code is perfect. The user wanted something different.
YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It): YAGNI: trust the process, not the future-proofing.
You aren't gonna need it. You won't. Trust.
We added extensibility for features that don't exist.
YAGNI: the most ignored principle in software development.
We built for every possible case. Needed only the simplest one.
KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid): Complexity is the enemy of reliability.
Simplicity is not laziness. It is craft.
The simplest solution is usually the correct one.
Clever code is a liability. Simple code is an asset.
DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself): Don't repeat yourself. Don't repeat yourself.
Every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous representation.
Three is too many. Two is already suspicious.
Copy-paste is the opposite of DRY. So is your codebase.
DRY: violated 47 times in this file alone.
WET (Write Everything Twice): WET: Write Everything Twice. Or more.
WET code: where copy-paste is a design decision.
This is the fourth implementation of the same logic.
SOLID principle puns: Single Responsibility: one class, one job, zero regrets.
Open/Closed: open for extension, closed for modification, open for debate.
Liskov Substitution: if it quacks like a duck, it must not explode when used as a duck.
Interface Segregation: many small interfaces, much confusion.
Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions. Abstract everything.
Design pattern references: We need a factory for our factories.
Have you considered an observer for your observer?
It's patterns all the way down.
Apply the pattern. Then spend three days explaining it.
The pattern is the solution. Finding the pattern is the problem.
Singleton anti-pattern jokes: There can only be one. Unfortunately.
The Singleton: impossible to test, easy to regret.
getInstance() // called 47 times from 23 different files
Factory pattern confusion: We need a factory to decide which factory to use.
AbstractFactory: a factory of factories for creating factories.
I created a factory. For what? Nobody knows.
The factory produces objects. And confusion.
Factory method: for when 'new' is too much commitment.
Observer pattern metaphors: Everyone is subscribed. Nobody remembers subscribing.
Memory leak: the observer who forgot to unsubscribe.
Observer: I will watch and do nothing. Until event.
The callback fires twice. The observer pattern promises nothing.
Builder pattern puns: Builder: for when your constructor needs a construction permit.
Build the thing. Step by step. Or not at all.
The builder pattern: for fluent interfaces and deep confusion.
We used builder. Now we build builders.
Decorator pattern jokes: It's just a wrapper. On top of a wrapper. On top of the original thing.
Add behavior without changing the underlying class... or sanity.
Decorator: Russian nesting dolls for code.
I decorated the decorator. Performance unknown.
Five layers of decoration. One layer of functionality.
Adapter pattern confusion: We adapted the adapter so it could adapt other adapters.
Adapter: making incompatible things compatible since forever.
The adapter is thicker than the original code.
Wrap it. Rename it. Pretend it's different.
Adapters: duct tape of the software world.
Proxy pattern metaphors: The proxy handles it. Or pretends to handle it.
Proxy: a polite name for man-in-the-middle.
Real object? No. But a really good fake.
The proxy pattern: for when you want an impersonator.
Strategy pattern puns: Strategy: choose your algorithm at runtime. Choose wisely.
Strategy A failed. Fall back to Strategy B. And C. And panic.
Inject the strategy. Extract the algorithm. Ship the confusion.
The strategy changed. So did all the tests.
We have several strategies. None of them are working.
State pattern jokes: The system is in an unknown state. This is always true.
State machine: for those who enjoy drawing state diagrams.
Every object has state. Not every developer knows it.
Command pattern references: Encapsulate a request as an object. Then lose the object.
Undo supported. Please note undo is broken.
Command queue: where requests go to wait.
The command fired. The handler was not listening.
Command pattern: for when a function call feels too simple.
Visitor pattern confusion: The visitor visited every node. Left no comment.
Visitor: accepting the visitor pattern requires accepting confusion.
Double dispatch: not actually double the fun.
The visitor knows everything about the structure. Nobody knows the visitor.
accept(visitor) // what has been accepted into your codebase
Template method puns: The template defines the steps. The subclass ignores them.
Override the hook. Or don't. The template doesn't care.
The skeleton is defined. The flesh is filled in by someone else.
Inversion of control: the parent calls the child calls the parent.
Chain of responsibility jokes: Pass it up the chain. Someone else will handle it.
None of the handlers handled it.
Chain of responsibility: for when nobody wants to be responsible.
The request traveled the entire chain. Returned unhandled.
Bridge pattern metaphors: Abstraction and implementation: finally separated. Still confused.
Bridge: for when inheritance isn't complicated enough.
Separate the what from the how. Then forget which is which.
The bridge is solid. The concepts it bridges are not.
Flyweight pattern puns: Share state. Save memory. Sacrifice clarity.
Intrinsic vs extrinsic state. One is shared. One is yours. Good luck.
So many objects, so little memory.
Flyweight: doing more with less, confusingly.
Memento pattern references: Save state without breaking encapsulation. Memento approves.
The memento stores everything it remembers. Including your mistakes.
Originator, Caretaker, Memento. A cast of many, a pattern few understand.
Interpreter pattern jokes: We wrote a mini-language. Nobody asked for a mini-language.
The interpreter pattern: for when you want to build a language inside a language.
Grammar defined. Sentences still don't make sense.
The expression tree is deep, dark, and recursive.
If your solution involves an interpreter, the problem was simpler than you thought.
Mediator pattern metaphors: Everyone talks to the mediator. The mediator talks to everyone. Peace?
Mediator: the middleware nobody wanted but everyone needed.
Reducing coupling by creating a single point of coupling.
The mediator knows too much. But at least it's organized.
Think of the mediator as middle management for your objects.
Iterator pattern puns: The iterator iterates. Nobody knows what over.
for each: the iterator you forgot was an iterator.
Iterator: walk the collection without knowing the collection.
Composite pattern jokes: Treat individual objects and groups of objects uniformly. Sure.
Is it a leaf? Is it a composite? Does it matter?
The tree is recursive. So are the bugs.
Composite pattern: where one object is indistinguishable from many.
Add a child. Add a child's child. Add regret.
Facade pattern references: Behind the facade, chaos. Above it, peace.
The public API is clean. You don't want to see the implementation.
Facade: a polite lie about the complexity within.
Ten services, one interface. That's a facade.
The facade holds. The internals do not.
